content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
1 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Ashley K. Dunning 
Nossaman LLP 
50 California Street, 34th Floor 
San Francisco, CA 94111 
May 9, 2017 
Re: 
Your Request for lnfonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-093 
Dear Ms. Dunning: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Marin County Employees' 
Retirement Association ("MCERA") Board of Retirement Member Steven Silberstein regarding the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only 
providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general 
conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 2 
Because your question is general in nature and does not identify a specific governmental 
decision, we are treating your request as one for infonnal assistance. Infonnal assistance does not 
provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 
83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Board Member Silberstein from taking part in governmental decisions 
regarding investment in funds of BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG specified in 
the facts, given that the Board Member has investments in each of those business entities valued 
between $10,001 to $ I ,000,000? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part in a decision regarding 
MCERA's investments in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG funds if that 
decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of those 
business entities, as discussed below. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 You confirmed that you are not seeking advice regarding Section l 090 on April 21, 2017. 


FACTS 
File No. I-17-093 
Page No. 2 
You are the authorized representative of the Marin County Employees' Retirement 
Association ("MCERA") and MCERA Board of Retirement Member Steven Silberstein. The Marin 
County Board of Supervisors recently appointed Mr. Silberstein (the "Board Member") to the 
Retirement Board. According to the Board Member's Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700), 
he has stock investments in BlackRock and State Street, each valued from $100,001 - $1,000,000, 
and in Invesco and UBS Group AG, each valued from $10,001 - $100,000. You state that the Board 
Member receives dividends and other payments as a result of his investments in these companies. 
MCERA contracts with BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, and UBS Group AG, among 
others, to access specific sectors of the financial markets or provide custom services. For example, 
BlackRock manages a Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ("TIPS") Index Fund, Invesco 
manages a commingled investment trust commodity fund, and State Street provides custodial 
services and access to an S&P 500 Index Fund through its subsidiary State Street Global Advisors. 
The Board Member's investments in these companies are at the broader company level rather than 
in the companies' specific funds or subsidiaries. 
You state that online reports provide the following net revenue figures for each of the 
companies as of December 31, 2016: BlackRock - $11.16 billion; Invesco - $1.19 billion; State 
Street - $10.64 billion; and UBS Group AG - $36.28 billion. You also state that the fair market 
value of MCERA's investments managed by each of the companies is approximately as follows: 
BlackRock TIPS Fund - $36.5 million; Invesco commodities fund - $30 million; State Street S&P 
Fund - $502 million; and UBS Group Core Real Estate Fund - $ 1 10. 7 million. 
Finally, you state that the Board Member is willing to consider disposing of some of his 
investments in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, and UBS Group AG to avoid those investments 
potentially giving rise to disqualifying conflicts of interest under the Act, but he would like to retain 
at least $2,000 in stock investments in each of those companies. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests 
identified in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the facts presented implicate 
the following interests of the Board Member: 
• Business Interest. An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(a).) The Board Member has business interests in BlackRock, Invesco, 
State Street, and UBS Group AG. 
Disposing of interests in those entities or creating a blind trust for investments can eliminate 
the potential for conflicts of interest under the Act. You state that the Board Member is willing to 
consider disposing of some of his investments in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, and UBS Group 


File No. 1-17-093 
Page No. 3 
AG to avoid those investments giving rise to disqualifying conflicts of interest under the Act, but he 
would like to retain at least $2,000 in stock investments in each of those businesses. We note that 
because an official who has invested $2,000 or more in a business entity's stock has an interest in 
that business entity pursuant to Section 87103(a), the Board Member would have to bring his 
investment in those business entities below $2,000 to not have an interest in the business entity 
under the Act. 
As to a blind trust, the general rule in Regulation 18234 provides that an official has an 
interest in the pro rata share of the interests in real property, sources of income, and investments of 
a trust in which the official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest of l 0 percent or greater. 
Regulation 18235 describes the attributes of a "blind trust" for purposes of the Act, and sets forth an 
exception to the provisions of Regulation 18234. 
Compliance with Regulation 18235 permits an official who is required to file SEI's to 
establish a blind trust and, thereby, to perform his or her official duties without worrying whether 
ownership of certain interests might give rise to conflicts of interest or disclosure obligations under 
the Act. (Turovsf..y Advice Letter, No. 1-06-141; see Donavan Advice Letter, No. A-02-078.) 
Compliance with Regulation 18235 is intended to shield an SEI "filer"3 from any knowledge 
regarding, or control of, some or all of the assets contained in a blind trust. 
• 
Source of Income Interest. An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more 
provided to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 
87103(c).) 
However, the Act's definition of "income" expressly excludes dividends, interest, or any 
other return on a security which is registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission of the 
United States government. (Section 82030(b)(5).) Assuming the only payments the Board Member 
receives from BlackRock, lnvesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG fall within this exclusion, the 
Board Member would not have source-of-income interests in those business entities due to his 
receipt of those payments. 
Furthermore, the definition of"income" also expressly excludes proceeds from the sale of 
securities registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission of the United States government 
if the filer sells the securities on a stock exchange and does not know or have reason to know the 
identity of the purchaser. (Section 82030(b)(l2).) If the Board Member disposes of his investment 
in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG in the manner described in this exclusion, 
he will not have a source-of-income interest in that business entity due to his receipt of the proceeds 
of the sale. 
• 
Interest in Personal Finances. An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an 
immediate family member. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her 
personal finances. 
3 Section 82026 states that "Filer" means the person filing or required to file any statement or report under the 
Act. The phrase "person filing" refers to a person acting as agent of a person "required to file" a statement or report 
under the Act. 


File No. 1-17-093 
Page No. 4 
Therefore, the Board Member has interests in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, and UBS 
Group AG as business entities, and an interest in his own personal finances and those of immediate 
family members.4 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
You have not provided facts concerning a specific decision, therefore we are generally 
discussing foreseeability and materiality. You may want to contact us if a specific issue arises. 
A decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding 
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, 
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to an official's interest in 
a business entity. Regulation 18702.1 (a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a 
decision on an official's interest in a business entity is material whenever the business entity does 
any of the following: 
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be made 
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government action 
concerning the business entity; 
"(2) Offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's agency; 
"(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency; 
"( 4) Is the named manufacturer in a purchase order of any product purchased 
by the official's agency or the sales provider of any products to the official's 
agency that aggregates to $1,000 or more in any 12-month period; 
"(5) Applies for a pennit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or 
other entitlement that the official's agency is authorized to issue; 
"(6) Is the subject of any inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the 
regulatory authority of the official's agency; or 
"(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the effect 
of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has an 
interest." 
_ Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and 
subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that ifa decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity, 
materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. Because decisions relating to BlackRock, Invesco, State 
Street, and UBS Group AG would affect the Board Member's interests in those business entities, we apply Regulation 
18702.1, and we do not further address the Board Member's interest in his personal finances or those of his immediate 
family members. 


File No. 1-17-093 
Page No. 5 
Thus, the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision identified in Regulation 18702.1 (a) on an 
official's interest in a business entity is material. 
If the official's interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701 (b) 
provides that the decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as 
a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
If a business interest is affected by a decision but not explicitly involved in the decision, 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard and provides as follows: 
"(b) For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial 
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions' financial effect would contribute to a 
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value 
of a privately-held business entity. Examples of decisions that may be 
applicable include those that: 
"(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business entity is engaged; 
"(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field for which the 
business entity is engaged; 
"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business 
entity supplies; 
"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that 
may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the 
business entity receives; 
"(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or 
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity 
draws from the location of the project; or 
"(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of 
the business entity." 
Thus, if a decision is not identified in Regulation 18702.1 (a), that decision is material if a prudent 
person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect 
would contribute to a change in the price of a business entity's publicly traded stock, or a privately 
held business entity's value. (Regulation 18702.l(b).) 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part 
in a decision relating to BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG, if that decision would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the Board Member's 
financial interests in those business entities. 


File No. I-1 7-093 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

between $10,001 to $ I ,000,000?

concerning the business entity; 
"(2) Offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's agency; 
"(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency; 
"( 4) Is the named manufacturer in a purchase order of any product purchased 
by the official's agency or the sales provider of any products to the official's

interest."

regarding investment in funds of BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG specified in 
the facts, given that the Board Member has investments in each of those business entities valued 
between $10,001 to $ I ,000,000?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act would prohibit the Board Member from taking part in a decision regarding 
MCERA's investments in BlackRock, Invesco, State Street, or UBS Group AG funds if that 
decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of those 
business entities, as discussed below.2 You confirmed that you are not seeking advice regarding Section l 090 on April 21, 2017.
